coredata.dc:title: Developing positively surface-charged carbon dots as “semiconductor electrolyte” for photo-electrochemical H2O2 production based on oxygen reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yong-Shuai
Duan, Hui-Min
Wang, Na
Wei, Dong-Dong
Mo, Liu-Meng
Wang, Ning
Wang, Xin-Pei
Lei, Shuang-Lei
Zhang, Yuan
Wang, Hong-Yan
coredata.dc:description: Electrolyte modulation for tailoring photoelectrochemical (PEC) activity and selectivity is highly effective but has proved challenging. The major obstacle is lack of a universal strategy that aims for improving the system charge separation based on electrolyte optimization. Herein, we presented an idea as “semiconductor electrolyte”, which was developed from the imidazole chloride delivered carbon dot (CDs), and featured for the positive surface charge. The CDs aqueous solution can accumulate H2O2 on a CuBi2O4 photocathode via PEC O2 reduction reaction (ORR) at neutral pH without any traditional electrolyte. Combined with experimental measurements and theoretical calculation, the mechanism of system and function of CDs was characterized. As electrolytes, CDs solution enabled to tune the energy band location of CuBi2O4. The electrostatic interaction between positively-charged CDs and negatively-charged photocathode triggered the fast charge and mass migration during PEC proceeding. As semiconductors, CDs can fabricate a dynamic p-n heterojunction with CuBi2O4. These advantages facilitated the photo-induced charge separation, which contributed to the boosted photocurrent and improved stability in PEC system. As ORR occurred interface that incorporated active sites, CDs provided a hydrophilic and adjusted-electronic surface, which fascinated to confine O2 and reduced the kinetic barrier for H2O2 generation. As a result, H2O2 generation was regulated with 0.44 mM and faraday efficiency over 84 % within 10 min, nearly 3 times to the commonly-used Na2SO4 solution. Our work therefore provides a striking and facile strategy to tailor PEC activity and selectivity in a durable and robust pathway. Importantly, it will evoke to develop a universal protocol for regulating PEC performance by electrolyte modulation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The HR-TEM image of CDs, and the inset displaying the lattice space of 0.21 nm related to the (100) plane of graphitic structure. (b) The XRD spectra of CDs. (c) The high-resolution C1s spectra. (d) The high-resolution N1s spectra of CDs in XPS measurement. (e) The Zeta potential of CDs. (f) The proposed formation process and structure of CDs. (g) UV–vis spectrum and (h) photoluminescence spectra of CDs samples in water. Insets show the images of CDs in water and the CDs aqueous solution irradiated by 365 nm light.

(a) The LSV of CuBi2O4 photocathode in O2-saturated CDs or Na2SO4 aqueous solution, and in Ar-saturated CDs or Na2SO4 aqueous solution in the dark or under irradiation of 395 nm LED lamp (the input optical power is 8.87 mW cm−2) respectively. (b) The yield of H2O2 and FE% for H2O2 generation in CDs solution. (c) The yield of H2O2 and FE% for H2O2 generation in Na2SO4 solution. RRDE voltammograms of CuBi2O4 photocathode in (d) O2-saturated CDs solution or (e) O2-saturated Na2SO4 aqueous solution under irradiation. (f) The electron transfer number n and selectivity of CuBi2O4 photocathode at various potentials in O2-saturated CDs or Na2SO4 aqueous solution, calculated from RRDE data recorded at 2,000 rpm.

(a) IPCE of CuBi2O4 photocathode in O2-saturated CDs and Na2SO4 aqueous solution under different excitation wavelength at 0.2 V respectively. (b) EIS of CuBi2O4 photocathode in O2-saturated CDs and Na2SO4 aqueous solution in the dark or under irradiation. (c) OCVD plots of CuBi2O4 photocathode in O2-saturated CDs and Na2SO4 aqueous solution in the dark or under irradiation from the electrolyte–electrode side (ΔV is the difference in voltage between dark and illumination conditions). (d) Mott-Schottky plots of CuBi2O4 photocathode in O2-saturated CDs and Na2SO4 aqueous solution. (e) The determined energy levels of CuBi2O4 photocathode in O2-saturated CDs aqueous solution. (f) The proposed process for PEC ORR on CuBi2O4 photocathode in CDs solution.

(a) The UV–vis spectra for the mixture of PEC ORR system in the presence or absence of NBT based on DPD/POD detection method. The contact angles of (b) CDs sample and (c) CuBi2O4 substrate in water droplets experiments. (d) Free energy diagrams for PEC ORR on CuBi2O4 or the heterojunction between CDs and CuBi2O4 for H2O2 production, in which black line represents the energy for the pristine CuBi2O4 in Na2SO4 solution, red line represents the energy for the heterojunction with N nearest neighbor C atom on CDs as the active sites, and blue line represents the energy for the heterojunction with Cu on CuBi2O4 as the active sites. Preferred *OOH absorption configurations based on DFT studies for pristine (e) CuBi2O4, in which O was connected with Cu, (f) the heterojunction of CDs and CuBi2O4, in which O binds with N nearest neighbor C atom on CDs particles, (g) the heterojunction of CDs and CuBi2O4, in which O was combined with Cu atom on CuBi2O4. Yellow, red, grey, blue, black, violet, and green spheres represent hydrogen, oxygen, carbon, nitrogen, chlorine, bismuth, and, copper respectively.

head.ce:title: Developing positively surface-charged carbon dots as “semiconductor electrolyte” for photo-electrochemical H2O2 production based on oxygen reduction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrolyte modulation for tailoring photoelectrochemical (PEC) activity and selectivity is highly effective but has proved challenging. The major obstacle is lack of a universal strategy that aims for improving the system charge separation based on electrolyte optimization. Herein, we presented an idea as “semiconductor electrolyte”, which was developed from the imidazole chloride delivered carbon dot (CDs), and featured for the positive surface charge. The CDs aqueous solution can accumulate H2O2 on a CuBi2O4 photocathode via PEC O2 reduction reaction (ORR) at neutral pH without any traditional electrolyte. Combined with experimental measurements and theoretical calculation, the mechanism of system and function of CDs was characterized. As electrolytes, CDs solution enabled to tune the energy band location of CuBi2O4. The electrostatic interaction between positively-charged CDs and negatively-charged photocathode triggered the fast charge and mass migration during PEC proceeding. As semiconductors, CDs can fabricate a dynamic p-n heterojunction with CuBi2O4. These advantages facilitated the photo-induced charge separation, which contributed to the boosted photocurrent and improved stability in PEC system. As ORR occurred interface that incorporated active sites, CDs provided a hydrophilic and adjusted-electronic surface, which fascinated to confine O2 and reduced the kinetic barrier for H2O2 generation. As a result, H2O2 generation was regulated with 0.44 mM and faraday efficiency over 84 % within 10 min, nearly 3 times to the commonly-used Na2SO4 solution. Our work therefore provides a striking and facile strategy to tailor PEC activity and selectivity in a durable and robust pathway. Importantly, it will evoke to develop a universal protocol for regulating PEC performance by electrolyte modulation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: As a green redox reagent and potential energy carrier, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has the diverse application in chemical industry, environmental treatment, green synthesis as well as fuel cell technology [1]. At present, the synthesis of H2O2 chemicals heavily relies on the traditional anthraquinone oxidation method [2]. It is well-established, but the inherent complexity associated with high energy consumption has pushed the researchers to find out more simple and direct approach for H2O2 production. Among them, O2 reduction reaction (ORR) based on photoelectrochemical (PEC) technology enabling to convert renewable solar energy into high-valuable chemicals, is regarded as one of the best choices to aim above demands [3]. However, this strategy meets great challenges. It is because hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) or 4e− ORR route into H2O on the photocathode strongly competes with 2e− ORR pathway to form H2O2 [4]. Additionally, the sluggish kinetics of ORR greatly hinders the efficiency of H2O2 production [5]. Therefore, only a few practical and effective protocols are approached for H2O2 generation based on PEC ORR until now [3,6]. In order to accumulate H2O2, a suitable photocathode is of vital importance, which not only needs to fascinate PEC ORR with the satisfied activity and durability, but also enables to regulate the catalytic selectivity prone to H2O2 generation. CuBi2O4 possessing relatively narrow band gap, appropriate energy band location, and positive photocurrent onset potential, has been identified as the most-extensively studied p-type semiconductor photocathode for H2O2 production [6]. Importantly, after fabricating the CuBi2O4 via nanostructuring [6a–b], building heterojunctions [6c] or heteroatom doping [6d], the photocathode can feature for a dramatically enhanced charge separation and smoothly furnish the fundamental ORR into the H2O2 generation pathway.

Compared with photocathode design and its fabrication, the modulation of catalytic media adds more convenience and effectiveness to prompt the competency for PEC ORR [7]. It has been noticed that the electrolyte functionalizes in charge migration, and balances the charge in the catalytic system. The interaction of electrolyte and photoelectrode interface can even tune the band edge location of semiconductor, and further implicates the charge injection and quality of photocurrents, leading to a significant change in interface catalysis [8]. Therefore, fine-tuning of the electrolyte turns out to be an effective protocol to tailor PEC performance, but it has proved challenging. One reason was originated from the great complexity in electrolyte optimization, in which too many factors, including electrolyte composition, concentration, pH, conductivity and so forth, should be fully taken account [8]. Another more serious obstacle lies in the lack of a universal protocol to prompt the key step, namely photo-induced charge separation in PEC process based on electrolyte optimization. In 2021, we pioneered to use EDTA·2Na generated carbon dots (CDs) as reaction medias for PEC water oxidative O2 production on α-Fe2O3 photoanodes, which indicated the superior efficiency and durability to normal electrolytes such as Na2SO4 and NaOH with identical pH and electroconductibility [9]. Very recently, a series of CDs derived from linear aliphatic amino acids were developed by us. In such CDs aqueous solution, it achieved a pronounced synergy not only in regulating a precise 2e− pathway for PEC water oxidative H2O2 generation, but also efficiently suppressing its decomposition on a BiVO4 photoanode [10]. It appeared that the CDs in above two cases were covered by the negatively-charged groups. We assumed that the electrostatic attraction between CDs and n-type semiconductor photoanode can trigger the directional movement of charged CDs, which created internal circuit. After CDs approaching to the photoanode, the aligned energy structures allowed them to assemble into a heterojunction for charge separation, which was essential for the enhancement of PEC performance. Inspired by this, we proposed that to employ the positively surface-charged CDs as reaction media, in combination with p-type CuBi2O4 photocathode, may match to promote PEC ORR activity based on the similar principle. More importantly, well-designed CDs can incorporate a variety of oxygen functional groups, with the capability to optimize active sites and turn the selectivity [11]. Therefore, it is possible to harness ORR to mediate a 2e− pathway into much more useful H2O2 production. Here, we envision a novel positively surface-charged CDs generated from imidazole chloride, and in the CDs aqueous solution without any other traditional electrolyte, CuBi2O4 photocathode can deliver a photocurrent reaching up to 2.5 mA cm−2 at 0 V (vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE)) under an O2 saturated condition, and yield H2O2 with the faraday efficiency (FE%) over 84 %. This performance is far ahead of that in Na2SO4 solution under the same conditions. Moreover, the involved mechanism was well-studied in the combination of experimental measurement and theoretical calculation. Based on these achievements and our previous observations [9,10], we developed an idea as “semiconductor electrolyte”, in which surface-charged CDs were employed and functioned as both semiconductors and electrolytes. This idea may dedicate to establishing a feasible and universal protocol for the regulation of PEC selectivity and activity based on electrolyte optimization. Our research thus can lay foundation for developing highly-performed PEC systems with diverse promising applications.
The CuBi2O4 covered FTO photocathode was prepared according to the method reported in the literatures [12], which was characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) shown in Fig. S1.

Hydrothermal method was used to prepare CDs with 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride as the precursor and N source [13]. Typically, 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride (1.75 g) was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water to deliver 1 M aqueous solution, which was then kept in an autoclave at 220 °C for 8 h. The obtained system was filtered through a filter with the diameter of 0.22 μm. Then the resulted solution was dialyzed by a cellulose dialysis membrane with molecular weight cut off (MWCO) of 500 Da in 1 L of deionized water under subsequent stirring. Every 4 h, fresh deionized water was applied and the whole dialysis process lasted for 2 days. The obtained solution was frozen-dried, which delivered 0.35 g CDs as a light-yellow powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The PEC measurement and detection of product
ce:section.ce:para: The details for PEC measurement and detection of H2O2 were described in the supplementary materials.

The preparation of CDs was applied with 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride as the precursor and N source via hydrothermal treatment at 220 °C for 8 h [13]. After a series of purification and frozen-drying, CDs was delivered as a light yellow powder. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) demonstrated the fabricated CDs featured as spherical nanoparticles with uniform diameters of 3–6 nm. Both the graphitic and amorphous carbons were detected, typically with lattice fringes in the graphitic regions corresponding to (100) interlayer spacing of 2.1 Å in CDs (Fig. 1 a) [14]. XRD spectra (Fig. 1b) verified the nano-crystallization and functionalization of CDs, with a detection of a broad diffraction peak centered at 23.8°. This signal correlated with the lattice spacing of 3.7 Å, indicating disordered graphitic-type structure was involved [14a,15]. The formation of CDs can also be monitored by NMR spectra, in which the signals for CDs was expected to split into several pricks compared to its precursor imidazole chloride in 13C NMR spectrum, accompanied by the emergence of broad and non-resolved proton peaks in 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S2) [16]. Next, we characterized the surface groups of CDs by the union of several methods. As shown in infrared (IR) spectroscopy (Fig. S3), CDs exhibited two clear stretches of C-H groups on imidazole ring at ∼ 3145 and 3094 cm−1 [17]. At ∼ 1567 cm−1, a vibrations of imidazole framework emerged, which was associated with the stretching signal at ∼ 1169 cm−1, suggesting imidazole ring was kept after the generation of CDs [17]. The Fig. 1c depicted the high-resolution C1s of CDs in X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which was deconvoluted into two peaks. The lowest energy contribution at 284.60 eV evidenced the emergence of sp2 C = C environment, accompanied by the signals at 285.70 eV corresponding to C-N or C = N groups [14a,18]. In high-resolution of N1s spectrum (Fig. 1d), the peak centered at 401.38 eV was ascribed to graphitic N, along with the respective contributions of pyrrolic N and pyridinic N at 400.21 eV and 398.81 eV, with the atomic ratio of 85.92 %, 6.20 % and 7.88 % respectively [14a,19]. Additionally, we examined the Zeta potential of CDs (Fig. 1e). The positive value of 8.25 mV strongly evidenced that the CDs surface was positively charged [20]. After summing up the characterized data, the composition and structure of CDs was proposed in Fig. 1f, in which the quaternary ammomium imidazole ring was remained and covered on CDs surface, and both imidazole ring and alkyl chain converted into N-doped graphitic-type structure after decomposition, polymerization and carbonization for CDs formation [13]. The CDs samples can dissolve into water instantaneously and afforded a transparent light yellow solution. Characterized by UV–vis absorption, it displayed a typical band related to conjugated π → π* (C = C) structure, which ranged from 300 to 360 nm, along with a tail extending to the visible light region (Fig. 1g) [21]. Read from Fig. 1h, CDs performed the featured excitation-wavelength dependent behavior, showing the emission maximum shifted from 413 to 501 nm with the excitation wavelength moving from 345 to 425 nm [21a,22]. Therefore, it is distinct that CDs can be activated by visible light, which can implicate great influence on PEC activity [23].

The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was initiated to examine the PEC ORR performance on CuBi2O4 photocathode in the O2 saturated CDs solution at a neutral pH (pH = 6.8 with electroconductibility at 45 mS cm−1) (Fig. 2 a, Fig. S4). All the potentials were versus RHE without further specification. In the dark, the catalytic systems were almost silent ether in the presence of O2 or in its absence, while under the constant irradiation of blue-light LEDs (λ = 395 nm), the photocurrent was initiated, which approached to 0.62 mA cm−2 at 0 V without O2. The combination of light-irradiation and O2 can bring out the pronounced photo-electronic response, which was started at 1.0 V and entailed a sustained increase over the entire testing potential range, achieving 2.14 mA cm−2 at 0 V. Evidently, photo-reduction of O2 is thermodynamically and kinetically much easier than water reduction on the photocathodes in CDs solution [3a,24]. In stark contrast, PEC ORR was seriously suppressed after switching CDs solution into Na2SO4 aqueous solution with the identical electro-conductibility and pH (Table S1). The onset potential in Na2SO4 system was negatively moved to 0.85 V, along with the photocurrent density decreased to 1.1 mA cm−2. Under the chopped light, CDs solution can also deliver the higher photocurrent intensity based on ORR compared to Na2SO4 aqueous solution (Fig. S5). Long-term electrolysis was operated in CDs solution, which can afford a more stable photocurrent and panned over the long periods of time, suggesting the dramatically enhanced stability and durability (Figs. S6 and S7). Furthermore, the produced H2O2 was identified and quantified according to N,N-diethyl-1,4-phenylene-diamine/peroxidase (DPD/POP) method (Fig. S8) [25]. Noted that H2O2 was susceptible to temperature, the time course of photoelectrolysis for H2O2 generation was carried out in ice bath (below 5 °C) with continuous illumination at selected bias for 10 min [26]. As describe in Fig. 2 (b), the catalytic activity of CuBi2O4 photocathode in CDs solution for H2O2 generation exhibited a volcano dependence on the bias and reached the highest at 0 V (Fig. S9). Typically, the amount of H2O2 approached to 0.44 mM at 0 V, with the faraday efficiency (FE) expressed as 84 %. In comparison, the Na2SO4 system was far behand (Fig. S10), with the yield of H2O2 dramatically decreased to 0.15 mM at 0 V, accounting for the FE% as 46 % (Fig. 2c).

The selectivity for H2O2 generation in O2-saturated solution CDs or Na2SO4 solution with CuBi2O4 photocathode was further investigated by rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) test under the irradiation. As shown in Fig. 2(d) and (e), rising rotation speeds resulted into the gradually increased ring current in both CDs and Na2SO4 solution. However, CDs solution can afford larger ring current compared to Na2SO4 solution under the same potential and rotation speed, indicating that more H2O2 was produced on the photocathode [6d,24,27]. Considered the O2 mass transport can influence on experimental results, the extracted electron transfer number n and selectivity for H2O2 generation was evaluated at 2000 rmp. The value of n reached around 2.0 over the tested potential range in CDs solution, but higher than 3.1 obtained in Na2SO4 solution. This means that CDs solution dominated PEC ORR for 2e− H2O2 generation, but 4e− water formation was primary in Na2SO4 solution [6d]. Fig. 2(f) depicted the selectivity for H2O2 generation in both Na2SO4 solution and CDs solution. In combination, smaller n associated with a higher selectivity for H2O2 generation was displayed in CDs solution, which is consistent with the result for quantification of H2O2 based on the titration method [6d,27].

Fig. 3 (a) recorded the incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) in CDs and Na2SO4 solution with a bias at 0.2 V respectively. Both the cases displayed the similar profiles, in which the photo-electronic responses were generally decreased with the wavelength and silent at 630 nm, corresponding to the energy gap for CuBi2O4 of 1.97 eV [28]. It is distinct that CuBi2O4 photocathode in CDs solution can afford the higher conversion efficiency under each wavelength measurement, manifesting the better photo-generated electron-hole separation [29]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) shows the typical Nyquist plots of CuBi2O4 in CDs and Na2SO4 aqueous solution respectively, in which a depressed semicircle in high frequency region was detected, followed by a straight line in the low frequency region (Fig. 3b). The former semicircle represents the charge migration at the electrode/electrolyte interface, whereas, the followed straight line is attributed to the charge diffusion into the electrode material bulk [30]. CDs solution shows smaller impedance arc diameter than that in Na2SO4 solution under irradiation, evidencing the better charge separation stemmed from more facile charge transfer at the interface of CuBi2O4 photocathode and CDs electrolyte. This proposal was further supported by open circuit voltage decay (OCVD), a widely used method to evaluate charge transport in the systems [31]. Read from photovoltage-time (V-t) profiles depicted in Fig. 3(c), photovoltage ΔV of CuBi2O4 photocathode in CDs solution was expressed as 0.27 V, slightly higher than that in Na2SO4 solution with the value of 0.22 V. This was in line with the better charge separation [32]. Moreover, extracted from V-t profile, CuBi2O4 photocathode in CDs and Na2SO4 solution presented average charge decay lifetime of 1.54 s and 4.00 s respectively. Lower decay suggested CDs solution enables to deactivate the surface trap state more efficiently, so the photo-generated charge carriers can survive to participate in PEC conversion instead of being captured by surface trap, which leads to the dramatically enhanced PEC performance [33].

Since CDs solution can endow the PEC process on CuBi2O4 photocathode with the improved charge separation, the carrier density Nd and band location of photocathode in CDs solution were determined by Mott-Schottky measurement. In order to make the results more convincing, three different frequencies at 500 Hz, 750 Hz and 1000 Hz were applied (Fig. 3d and S11). Either in CDs or Na2SO4 solution, three M−S plots indicated almost the same intercept with potentials, but the curve in Na2SO4 solution displayed steeper slope that that of in CDs solution at each frequency. Typically, calculation based on slopes of Mott-Schottky curves at 1000 frequency afforded Nd with the respective value of 4.11 × 1016N cm−3 and 1.87 × 1016N cm−3 in CDs or Na2SO4 solution under dark conditions (equation S1) [34]. Visibly, the carrier density of CuBi2O4 photocathode was greatly increased after immersing into CDs solution. Such the enhancement was more impressive under irradiation, in which Nd of CuBi2O4 were 8.77 × 1016N cm−3 in CDs solution, four times of that in Na2SO4 solution with Nd of 2.31 × 1016N cm−3. Therefore, the greatly enhanced charge separation in CDs solution should be attributed to the increased charge density [35]. It is noted that both two solutions delivered the negative slopes in Mott-Schottky plots, meaning CuBi2O4 can keep the p-type semiconductor characterization [36]. As a typical p-type semiconductor, photo-generated holes were repelled from CuBi2O4 surface to the bulk of solid, leading to a negatively-charged photocathode surface to generate an extrinsic electric field, further stabilized by the components in surrounding electrolyte [37]. Therefore, the employed positively charged CDs can be attracted to move towards the interface of electron-accumulated photocathode plate under the electrostatic force during PEC proceeding, triggering the fast mass and charge transfer, accompanied by the establishment of internal circuit [38]. In this sense, the CDs solution can function as electrolytes. Based on Mott-Schottky measurement, flat band potential Vfb of the semiconductor can be determined [36,39]. Taken account of the negligible gap differences between Vfb and up edge of Valence Band (VB) for p-type semiconductor [36,39], it indicated the VB of CuBi2O4 was located at 1.34 V in CDs solution and 1.54 V in Na2SO4 solution respectively. As characterized in IPCE, CuBi2O4 photocathode possesses an energy gap (E g) of 1.97 eV, which entailed that respective Conduction Band (CB) was located at −0.63 V in CDs solution and −0.43 V in Na2SO4 solution. The HOMO and LUMO levels for CDs were further determined from the oxidation E ox and reduction potentials E red respectively in Cyclic Voltammetry, yielding the values of 1.59 V and −0.26 V respectively (Fig. S12) [40]. Fig. 3e summarized the energy alignment of CDs and CuBi2O4. Owing to the semiconductor feature of CDs, it allowed for the formation of a type II heterojunction between CDs and CuBi2O4 [41]. Teng et. al pointed out the embedding nitrogen atoms in the graphene frame can cause n-conductivity, which endowed the CDs with the properties of n-type semiconductors [42]. Accordingly, CuBi2O4 and the employed positively-charged CDs colloid particle may correspond to a p-n heterojunction. Thus, electrons migrated from CB of CuBi2O4 to LUMO of CDs, accompanied by the movement of holes from HOMO of CDs to VB of CuBi2O4 [39,43]. As a result, the charge separation was efficiently improved. The accumulated electrons were further extracted by O2 molecules to deliver H2O2 over the CDs surface, while holes diffuse to counter electrode through the external circuit for water oxidation (Fig. 3f). In this sense, CDs can be called as “semiconductor electrolyte”, which can function as both electrolytes and semiconductors.

Since O2 −• was responsible for H2O2 generation based on literature observation [44], we next tentatively monitor the possible intermediates involved for H2O2 generation in the CDs solutions. Owing to the fast degradation of O2 −• in aqueous solution, the direct detection of it is difficult. Therefore, nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT), regarded as a useful probe for O2 −• was employed [45]. It is perceived that NBT can capture O2 −• efficiently, which inhibits O2 −• to further convert into H2O2. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the yield of H2O2 in PEC ORR system was sharply decreased to 52 % in the presence of NBT compared to its absence (Fig. 4a), which manifested that O2 −• was involved in the process of PEC ORR.

Based on literation observations, the oxygen reduction into H2O2 comprises two proton coupled electron transfer (PCET) processes as fellows [46],

in which the first one (equation (1) represents the absorption of O2 on the active site “*” to create the key intermediate *OOH, and the last one (equation (2) indicates the dissociation of *OOH for the generation of H2O2. An ideal catalytic system for H2O2 synthesis should composite a hydrophilic surface, in which it is not only essential for capturing H2O, but also fascinates to confine and activate O2 [47]. Additionally, it should minimize the kinetic barrier between equation (1) and equation (2) to provide high activity and selectivity [46b,48]. Our above analysis demonstrated that the much better performances for PEC ORR in CDs solution resulted from the improved charge separation. Another reason corresponds to the proposal that the ORR preferred to occur on CDs surface rather than CuBi2O4 photocathode [49]. In order to confirm this proposal, the water droplets experiments were operated. As shown in Fig. 4(b) and (c), the CDs samples showed a contact angel of 28 °, while CuBi2O4 gave a value of 93 °. Accordingly, CDs possess a more hydrophilic surface than CuBi2O4 substrate, which endows CDs with the advantages to fine-tune the PEC activity and selectivity [50]. To further dissect the enhancement of H2O2 formation in CDs solutions, the Density-Functional Theory (DFT) calculation was performed to obtain the free energy diagram in 2e− pathway for ORR at U = 0.7 vs. RHE [48,51]. As shown in Fig. 4(d), it is 1.95 eV uphill to form *OOH for pristine CuBi2O4 (211) substrate by combining Cu atom with O2 (Fig. 4e). After uniting into heterojunction with CDs in CDs solution, O2 is much more favorable to bind on the N nearest neighbor C atom on CDs (Fig. 4f-g). This is because the free energy was −0.69 eV to form *OOH on CDs, but it was down shift to −5.27 eV on CuBi2O4 (211). Based on DFT calculation, two points can be summarized up. The first one is that CDs system behaviors differently from Na2SO4 solution for the generation of H2O2, in which the dissociation of *OOH process was the rate-determining step in CDs solution, but it was the formation of *OOH instead in Na2SO4 solution [52]. It is clear that the formation of *OOH on pristine CuBi2O4 in Na2SO4 solution is energy exhausted (positive value), while this process is thermodynamically favorable after forming a heterojunction in CDs solution (negative value) (Fig. 4d-f). Therefore, the formation of *OOH intermediate in CDs solution is much easier. Secondly, in CDs solution, although both CuBi2O4 and CDs can act as the active center to reduce O2, the energy requirement for the dissociation of *OOH on CDs is smaller than that on CuBi2O4 (Fig. 4d, f-g) (0.69 eV compared with 5.27 eV). It means that *OOH intermediate is easier to detach from the CDs surface than that from CuBi2O4 substrate. Therefore, CDs can provide higher reactivity and selectivity for H2O2 formation. We assumed it should be ascribed to the electronic effect of N nearest neighbor C atom on CDs. Such the C atom possessed the moderate charge, which not only favored to be filled, thus fascinated for the CO bonding, but also allowed the bond energy of CO not strong enough, and facilitated the H2O2 to be easily extricated from the catalytic surface [53]. This proposal was well supported by the bader charge analysis shown in Fig. S13 [54].

We have successfully designed and implemented a new type of PEC ORR system based on the electrolyte innovation. The system employed positively surface-charged CDs aqueous solution and converted oxygen to a valuable chemical H2O2 over CuBi2O4 photocathode with the yield of 0.44 mM and FE% over 84 % at 0 V within 10 min. This performance was greatly surpassed Na2SO4 electrolyte under the almost identical conditions. In the combination experimental observations and theoretical calculation, it demonstrated that the application of CDs solution in PEC system featured for the following advantages: (1) the static interaction between charged CDs and photoelectrode substrate during PEC proceeding can induce the fast mass and charge transfer in solution; (2) band edge configuration between the CDs and photoelectrode substrate, associated with their surface electrical behaviors allows for a fabrication of dynamic heterojunction in CDs solutions, which favored for the efficient charge separation and improved the PEC activity and stability; (3) CDs can adjust electronic structure and hydrophilic property of catalytic interface; (4) CDs acted as the active center to reduce O2, which facilitated the kinetic barrier for H2O2 generation to be distinctly decreased. Assisted by these achievements, a new idea of “semiconductor electrolyte” was developed, which indicated the surface-charged CDs can feature for both semiconductor and electrolyte in PEC system. Based on this idea, it suggest that the match of CDs electrolyte with proper photoelectrode can provide a universal protocol to regulate PEC activity and selectivity by electrolyte modulation, which can be further expanded to other PEC system, such as CO2 reduction, N2 reduction, full water splitting and so forth. The related research is on-going now.
